column10: Question: In its loss function YoloV3 uses logistic regression with multilabel classification or Softmax over all class probabilities? [YoloV3 uses logistic regression with multilabel classfication for its class predictions. Instead of a softmax function, which is typically used for multiclass classification. YoloV3 employs independent independent logistic classifiers for each class. This approach allows for more flexibility in handling multiple object classes within a single bounding box.]
column11: Question: In its loss function YoloV3 uses logistic regression with multilabel classification or Softmax over all class probabilities? [YOLOv3 uses logistic regression with multilable classification. It employs independent logistic classifier for each class, rather than a softmax function. This approach is chosen because the authors found it to be sufficient for achieving good performance without the need for softmax normalization accross all classes. The loss function used is binary cross-entropy, suitable for multilabelclassification tasks]

column17: Question: When defining the reading comprehension task, the authors explain that they wish to estimate p(a|c, q). What would a model trained on this task do if the context "c" itself had factually incorrect information? [A model trained on p(a ｜ c, q) would likely produce an answer "a" that is consistent with the factually incorrect information presented incontext "c". The model learns to predict the answer based on the relationships between the query "q" and the provided context, regardless ofwhether the context itself is accurate. This highlights the importance of evaluating reading comprehension models on datasets with factuallycorrect information to assess their true understanding. ]

column20: Question: When defining the reading comprehension task, the authors explain that they wish to estimate p(a|c, q). However, if the context "c" contains factuallyincorrect information, the model would still generate an answer based on that incorrect information, as it is not designed to verify theaccuracy of the context. The model\\'s performance in such cases would depend on how well it has learned to understand the linguisticrelationships between entities in the context document, regardless of the factual accuracy of the context.]
column21: Question: When defining the reading comprehension task, the authors explain that they wish to estimate p(a|c, q). Therefore, if the context “c” has incorrect information, the model is likely to answerbased on the factually incorrect information itself. The authors clearly explain that the task their model is being built for and evaluated on is ofidentifying answers from a given text (i.e. comprehension) and not knowledge of global correctness.]
column22: Question: When defining the reading comprehension task, the authors explain that they wish to estimate p(a|c, q). What would a model trained on this task do if the context "c" itself had factually incorrect information? [A model trained on this task would likely produce an answer "a" that is conditioned on the incorrect information present in the context "c", asit is designed to estimate the conditional probability p(a ｜ c, q). This means that the model would not have the ability to correct the factualerrors in the context, but rather would provide an answer based on the incorrect information. This could lead to an incorrect answer, even ifthe model has correctly understood the linguistic relationships between entities in the context document. The model's    	 	 	 	  performance wouldbe influenced by the quality of the context document, and it would not be able to distinguish between correct and incorrect information.]
column23: Question: When defining the reading comprehension task, the authors explain that they wish to estimate p(a|c, q). Since the model is designed to estimate theprobability of an answer (a) given a context (c) and a query (q), it would base its response on the information provided in "c", even if thatinformation is factually incorrect. The model doesn't have the capacity to fact-check or verify the information in the context.]
